title: Object Navigation
description: Learn how to explore and interact with controls that cannot be reached with the Tab key, using NVDA's object navigation commands.

steps.instruction: Welcome to the Object Navigation chapter. Most of what you have learned so far uses the Tab key and arrow keys to move through a screen. But some programs — especially older desktop applications — have controls that Tab cannot reach. NVDA has a separate system for exploring these called object navigation. It lets you move through every element on the screen, whether or not it accepts keyboard focus. Before you begin: maximize your current application window if it is not already full screen. Press Windows+Up Arrow (Windows command) to maximize. This ensures all objects are visible and accessible to the navigator. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Object navigation is separate from Tab navigation. Tab only reaches controls designed to accept keyboard focus. Object navigation reaches everything on screen.
Older desktop applications — like some media players, custom toolbars, and accessibility tools — often have controls that do not respond to Tab. Object navigation is how you reach them.
To maximize your window before exploring, press Windows+Up Arrow. A maximized window ensures no objects are hidden off-screen or clipped.

steps.instruction: NVDA thinks of the screen as a tree of objects — picture a pyramid. At the top is the application window. The level below it contains panels, toolbars, and menus. The level below that contains the individual controls: buttons, text fields, and checkboxes. Each object may contain more objects at the level below it. Object navigation lets you move through this pyramid in any direction: across to the next object at the same level, up one level to the container, or down one level to the first object inside. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Think of the pyramid like a building. The whole building is the application. Each floor is a level. Each room on a floor is a panel or toolbar. Each item in a room is an individual control.
Every element on screen — a toolbar button, a status bar, a progress indicator — lives somewhere in this pyramid, even if Tab cannot reach it.
Object navigation is most useful in programs that have non-standard controls — custom toolbars, status bars, notification areas, and embedded controls.

steps.instruction: There are two keyboard layouts in NVDA: desktop and laptop. Desktop layout uses the numeric keypad on the right side of a full-size keyboard. Laptop layout uses combinations of NVDA plus letter or symbol keys so you do not need a numpad. All object navigation commands in this chapter will show both layouts. If you are not sure which layout you are using, open the NVDA menu with NVDA+N (NVDA command), go to Preferences, then Settings, then Keyboard, and listen for the layout setting. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout uses numpad keys for object navigation. Laptop layout adds Shift to many arrow key combinations. All commands in this chapter list both.

If you are not sure which layout you are using, press NVDA+1 (NVDA command) to turn on Input Help, then press one of the object navigation keys to hear which layout NVDA expects. Then press NVDA+1 again to turn Input Help off.
You can change your keyboard layout at any time in NVDA Settings under the Keyboard category. The Customizing NVDA chapter covers this in detail.

lessons.title: Move to the Next and Previous Object

steps.instruction: To move to the next object at the same level in the pyramid, press NVDA+NumPad6 (desktop layout) or NVDA+Shift+Right Arrow (laptop layout). These are NVDA commands. To move to the previous object at the same level, press NVDA+NumPad4 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Left Arrow (laptop). NVDA announces each object as you land on it — its name, type, and sometimes its value. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: hold NVDA and press NumPad6 — the right arrow key on the numpad — to move to the next object. NumPad4 — the left arrow on the numpad — moves to the previous object.
Laptop layout: hold NVDA and Shift together, then press Right Arrow for the next object, or Left Arrow for the previous object.
NVDA announces the name, type, and sometimes the value of each object as you land on it — similar to what NVDA+Tab tells you about focused controls.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press NumPad6 (desktop) or Shift+Right Arrow (laptop) to move to the next object. NVDA will announce what it is. Then hold NVDA and press NumPad4 (desktop) or Shift+Left Arrow (laptop) to move to the previous object. Try moving through a few objects in any open application to get the feel of it. Press Enter here when you are ready to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: NumPad6 is the right arrow key on the numeric keypad. Hold NVDA and press it to move to the next object at the same level.
Laptop layout: Hold NVDA and Shift at the same time, then press Right Arrow. Make sure you are pressing Shift — without it, the arrow keys control the review cursor instead.
Open any familiar application like File Explorer or Notepad and explore its object pyramid. Move through a few objects to get a sense of how they are organized.

steps.instruction: Notice that NVDA announces the name, type, and sometimes the value of each object — whether or not it has keyboard focus. This is an important distinction. NVDA+Tab (NVDA command) reads the control that currently has keyboard focus. Object navigation reads whatever object the navigator is pointed at — they are two separate things. You can use NVDA+Tab any time to check where keyboard focus is, and use object navigation to explore beyond it. Press Enter to continue.
hints: NVDA+Tab tells you about the focused control — the one your keyboard is currently on. Object navigation moves independently of keyboard focus.
Even if an object cannot receive keyboard focus, object navigation can still reach it and read it aloud.
You might use NVDA+Tab to check where you are, then use object navigation to explore nearby controls that are not reachable by Tab.

steps.instruction: To move up one level — to the container that holds the current object — press NVDA+NumPad8 (desktop layout) or NVDA+Shift+Up Arrow (laptop layout). These are NVDA commands. To move down one level — into the first object inside the current one — press NVDA+NumPad2 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Down Arrow (laptop). This is how you explore the pyramid in depth: climbing up to see the bigger picture, or descending to find controls nested inside a panel or toolbar. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: NumPad8 — the up arrow on the numpad — moves you up one level to the container. NumPad2 — the down arrow on the numpad — moves you down one level to the first object inside.
Laptop layout: NVDA plus Shift plus Up Arrow moves up one level. NVDA plus Shift plus Down Arrow moves down one level.
Moving up takes you to the container — the panel or toolbar that holds the current object. Moving down takes you into the first object inside the current container.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Hold NVDA and press NumPad8 (desktop) or Shift+Up Arrow (laptop) to move up one level. NVDA will announce the container. Then press NVDA+NumPad2 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Down Arrow (laptop) to move back down one level to the first object inside. Climbing up and down the levels helps you find controls that are grouped together inside panels or toolbars. Press Enter when you are ready to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: NumPad8 is the up arrow on the numpad. Hold NVDA and press it to move up one level.
Laptop layout: Hold NVDA and Shift together, then press Up Arrow to move up one level. Hold NVDA and Shift, then press Down Arrow to move down one level.
If you move up one level and want to go back in, press the move-down command to enter the level below. The first object down is the first item inside the current container.

steps.instruction: When you have navigated to an object, you can have NVDA read it. Press NVDA+NumPad5 (desktop layout) or NVDA+Shift+Comma (laptop layout) to hear the full description of the current navigator object. These are NVDA commands. The comma key is in the bottom row of letters, between M and the period key. Press the command twice quickly to spell out the object name character by character. This is useful when the name is unfamiliar or abbreviated. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: hold NVDA and press NumPad5 — the center key on the numpad — to read the current navigator object.
Laptop layout: hold NVDA and Shift, then press the comma key. The comma is in the bottom row of letters, between M and the period.
Press the command twice quickly to spell the object name one character at a time. Useful when the name is unfamiliar or abbreviated.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Navigate to any object using NVDA+NumPad6 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Right Arrow (laptop). Then press NVDA+NumPad5 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Comma (laptop) to hear the full description of that object. Press the command twice quickly to spell the name. When you are ready, press Enter to continue.
hints: First navigate to an object using the next-object command, then read it with NVDA+NumPad5 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Comma (laptop).
Pressing the read command twice quickly spells the object name one character at a time — very useful for unfamiliar names or abbreviations.
The comma key for laptop layout is in the bottom row of the keyboard, between M and the period key.

lessons.title: Move Keyboard Focus to a Navigator Object

steps.instruction: Once you have used object navigation to find a control, you can move keyboard focus to it so you can interact with it normally. Press NVDA+Shift+NumPadMinus (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+BackSpace (laptop) to route focus to the current navigator object. These are NVDA commands. If the object can accept focus, your keyboard focus will move to it and you can then press Enter, Space, or type as normal. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: hold NVDA and Shift, then press the numpad minus key — top right of the numpad — to route keyboard focus to the current navigator object.
Laptop layout: hold NVDA and Shift, then press Backspace — the large key above Enter on the right side of the keyboard.
Not all objects can accept keyboard focus. If routing focus does not work, the object may be display-only — you can read it but not interact with it.

steps.instruction: There is also a reverse command: press NVDA+NumPadMinus (desktop) or NVDA+BackSpace (laptop) — without Shift — to move the navigator object to wherever your keyboard focus currently is. This is useful when you want to start exploring from where you already are in the program, rather than starting from the top of the pyramid. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: NVDA plus NumPadMinus (without Shift) moves the navigator to the currently focused control — a good starting point for object exploration.
Laptop layout: NVDA plus Backspace (without Shift) does the same thing. Use this to sync the navigator with your current keyboard focus.
Use this command at the start of an object navigation session to begin exploring from where you are rather than searching from the top of the pyramid.

steps.instruction: Object navigation takes practice. The best way to get comfortable is to maximize a familiar program — like Notepad or File Explorer — and spend a few minutes exploring its pyramid. Move across objects at the same level, go up to containers, come back down, and read each object as you go. Over time, you will build a mental picture of how programs are structured at every level. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Start with File Explorer — it has a well-structured pyramid with clearly named folders, toolbars, and panels. Good for practice.
Notepad is another good practice environment. Its object pyramid is simple and predictable.
Maximize the application window first (Windows+Up Arrow) so all objects are visible. Then explore — do not worry about memorizing all commands at once.

steps.instruction: Object navigation is most useful in three situations. First, when a program has controls that Tab cannot reach — some toolbars, custom panels, and application-specific controls only appear in the object pyramid. Second, when you need to read something on screen that is not focused — like a status bar showing progress or a notification area. Third, when you want to explore the structure of a new program before deciding how to interact with it. A key tip: always maximize the application window first. A maximized window gives the object navigator the fullest view of all available objects. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Status bars and progress indicators are common objects that Tab cannot reach. Object navigation lets you read them without any keyboard focus.
Custom toolbars in older applications — media players, photo editors, and some office tools — often only respond to object navigation.
Maximize first (Windows+Up Arrow), then navigate. You will find more objects and have fewer surprises.

steps.instruction: Most everyday tasks — email, web browsing, word processing — do not require object navigation. Tab, arrow keys, and browse mode cover almost everything. Object navigation is an advanced tool you reach for when the standard methods fall short. Knowing it is there means no control on any screen is truly out of reach. Press Enter to continue.
hints: If Tab and browse mode get you through your daily work, that is completely normal. Object navigation is a specialist tool, not a daily necessity for most users.
When a sighted helper says there is a button on screen but Tab cannot reach it, object navigation is your answer.
As you gain experience, you will develop an instinct for when to switch to object navigation. Most new screen reader users rarely need it at first.

steps.instruction: You have completed the Object Navigation chapter! You now know how to move across objects at the same level, climb up to containers, descend into the level below, read any object, and route keyboard focus to it. When you are ready, press NVDA+Shift+C to open the lesson picker and continue to the next chapter: Customizing NVDA — where you will learn to adjust your keyboard layout and tune speech speed, pitch, voice, and volume. Press Enter to finish.
hints: The next chapter is Customizing NVDA. It covers how to adjust speech speed, pitch, voice, and keyboard layout — all without opening settings menus if you prefer.
Object navigation quick reference — desktop: NumPad4/6 across same level, NumPad8/2 up and down levels, NumPad5 to read. Laptop: NVDA+Shift+Arrow, NVDA+Shift+Comma to read.
NVDA Coach saves your progress automatically. All completed lessons are marked when you return.
